fields.brief_block: Additionally, the use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so, and the other party continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force OCGA § 16-3-21, Wainwright v. State, 305 Ga. 63 (2019), Powers v. State, 297 Ga. 345 (2015).
fields.opinion_block: And a defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification when the evidence is that the supposedly justified party was the aggressor. See OCGA § 16-3-21 (b) (3) (“A person is not justified in using force . . . if he . . . [w]as the aggressor ... unless he withdraws from the encounter and effectively communicates to such other person his intent to do so and the other, notwithstanding, continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force.”)
fields.finding_analysis: Powers v. State supports the proposition. The opinion expressly quotes and applies OCGA § 16-3-21 (b) (3), holding that “a defendant is not entitled to a jury instruction on justification when the evidence is that the supposedly justified party was the aggressor,” and reciting the statute's withdrawal-and-communication exception in language that tracks the brief's proposition.
